original_hypothesis.title: Bonding.unwrapToken allows anyone to force-convert arbitrary holders by burning their FERC20 and pulling agent tokens from pair
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: unwrapToken() is publicly callable with an arbitrary list of accounts. It burns each account's FERC20 balance via burnFrom() (Bonding is token owner) and then transfers agent tokens from the pair to that account. This enables forced conversion/griefing at scale and may break user expectations/strategies, and can be weaponized to trigger adverse tax/accounting/locking consequences in downstream systems.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is explicitly identified by Phase A as an access invariant violation. It is a concrete, on-chain, unprivileged call path that affects arbitrary users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public unwrapToken() loops accounts; calls token.burnFrom(acc,balance) then agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance) with no access control / consent check

preconditions: Bonding is the owner of the deployed FERC20 token (true if token created via new FERC20 inside Bonding)
PairAddress holds agentToken balance and has approved transfers or transferFrom is otherwise possible (depends on router.approval step during graduation)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unrestricted third-party can burn others' balances via owner privilege
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone can force conversion for any holder list (griefing/forced asset migration)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(msg.sender==acc) and no role/owner gate on unwrapToken

analysis_questions: Does agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance) always succeed (approval set correctly) and is the allowance unlimited?
Can this be used to indirectly drain agent tokens or only force-transfer to the same account being burned?
What downstream integrations assume FERC20 remains held (staking, governance, vesting, tax)?
validation_requirements: Test unwrapToken called by an arbitrary attacker for third-party holders post-graduation
Confirm allowance/approval path from router.approval(pairAddress, agentToken, address(this), ...)
Assess griefing impact and whether any value can be extracted via induced state changes

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After graduation, attacker scrapes holder addresses and calls unwrapToken(token, holders[]) to forcibly burn their FERC20 and move agent tokens to them, potentially breaking user positions and causing downstream losses/DoS.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Griefing/forced conversion; potential secondary losses if agent token differs in transferability/tax or if integrations rely on FERC20
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of graduated bonding tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Unclear; conversion is irreversible due to burn

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Yes (large holder lists may hit block gas limits; attacker can still target subsets)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor burn Transfer events + agentToken Transfer events

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on forced token conversion
validation_result.impact_summary: Any external caller can force-burn FERC20 tokens from arbitrary holders and convert them to agent tokens without holder consent, enabling griefing and unauthorized asset manipulation at scale.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `unwrapToken` function in Bonding.sol is public with zero access control. It accepts an arbitrary list of account addresses and, for each one, burns their entire FERC20 balance using the privileged `burnFrom` (which succeeds because Bonding is the FERC20 owner) and transfers agent tokens from the pair to them. No consent check (msg.sender == acc), no role gate, and no modifier restricts who can call this. The FERC20 token is created via `new FERC20(...)` inside Bonding, making Bonding the Ownable owner. During graduation, `_openTradingOnUniswap` calls `router.approval(pairAddress, agentToken, address(this), ...)` which grants Bonding allowance to spend the pair's agent tokens. Both preconditions for the attack are satisfied by normal protocol operation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The unwrapToken function at Bonding.sol has no access modifiers. FERC20 is deployed with Bonding as owner (constructor sets msg.sender as owner via Ownable). The pair approval for agent tokens is set during _openTradingOnUniswap via router.approval(). All three components are verified in source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Public function with no access control allows any caller to force-burn FERC20 tokens from arbitrary accounts and transfer agent tokens to them without consent.
onlyOwner check passes because Bonding contract is the FERC20 owner (deployed via `new FERC20(...)` in launchFor). This allows Bonding to burn any user's tokens without their approval.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: FERC20 token = new FERC20(string.concat("fun ", _name), _ticker, initialSupply, maxTx);
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: FERC20 constructor uses Ownable(msg.sender), making Bonding the owner with burnFrom privileges over all holders.
During graduation, pair approves Bonding to spend agent tokens, enabling the transferFrom in unwrapToken to succeed.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls Bonding.unwrapToken(srcTokenAddress, [victim1, victim2, ...]) - no access control check
2. Function reads tokenInfo and verifies tradingOnUniswap == true (graduated token)
3. For each victim account, reads FERC20 balance via token.balanceOf(acc)
4. Calls token.burnFrom(acc, balance) - succeeds because Bonding is FERC20 owner
5. Calls agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance) - succeeds because pair approved Bonding during graduation
6. Victim's FERC20 tokens are irreversibly burned and agent tokens are sent to them without their consent
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point is `unwrapToken` which is `public` with no modifiers. The function iterates over a caller-supplied `accounts` array. For each account with a non-zero FERC20 balance, it calls `FERC20.burnFrom(acc, balance)`. Since FERC20 was deployed by Bonding via `new FERC20(...)` (Bonding.sol line ~166), Bonding is the `owner()` per Ownable, so `onlyOwner` on `burnFrom` passes. The `agentToken.transferFrom(pairAddress, acc, balance)` succeeds because during `_openTradingOnUniswap` (Bonding.sol line ~258), `router.approval(pairAddress, agentToken, address(this), ...)` grants Bonding allowance over the pair's agent token balance.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The FERC20 ownership is set at token creation time within launchFor, and the pair approval is set during graduation - both are part of normal protocol flow.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: After a token graduates (tradingOnUniswap == true), an attacker enumerates FERC20 holders (via Transfer event logs or on-chain indexing). The attacker calls `Bonding.unwrapToken(tokenAddress, [holder1, holder2, ...])` in a single transaction. For each holder, their FERC20 balance is burned and agent tokens are transferred to them from the pair. The conversion is irreversible (burned tokens cannot be recovered). This can be done by anyone with no capital requirement and no permission. The attack is griefing by nature - it forces a state change on users' assets without their consent.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. Any holder of a graduated FERC20 token can have their tokens forcibly converted by any external party. This violates the fundamental principle that users control their own assets. Specific impacts include: (1) Forced asset migration without consent, (2) Potential tax/accounting consequences for affected users in jurisdictions where token swaps are taxable events, (3) Users who held FERC20 for strategic reasons (e.g., timing their conversion) lose that optionality, (4) The burn does not reduce totalSupply (separate bug in FERC20._burn), so the 1:1 conversion ratio may be economically incorrect, (5) If the agent token has different transferability properties, users could be locked into an unwanted position.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to unwrapToken: either `onlyOwner` or require `msg.sender == acc` for each account in the loop
Consider making unwrapToken only callable by the token holder themselves: `function unwrapToken(address srcTokenAddress) public { address acc = msg.sender; ... }`
If batch unwrapping is needed for operational purposes, restrict it to an admin/operator role
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bonding, FFactory, FRouter, FERC20 infrastructure. 2. Call launchFor to create a new token. 3. Buy enough to trigger graduation (reach gradThreshold). 4. Verify tradingOnUniswap == true. 5. From a separate EOA (attacker), call unwrapToken(tokenAddress, [holderAddress]) where holderAddress holds FERC20 tokens. 6. Verify holderAddress's FERC20 balance is now 0 and they received agent tokens without initiating any transaction.
uncertainties: The exact allowance amount set during graduation may be consumed by earlier unwrap calls, potentially causing later unwraps to fail if allowance is exhausted - but this doesn't prevent the initial forced conversions
Whether the agent token created by AgentFactoryV3 has any transfer restrictions that could cause the transferFrom to revert is unknown (AgentFactoryV3 source not provided)
additional_testing_needed: Verify the agent token allowance is sufficient for all holders (check if it's set to the exact pair balance or type(uint256).max)
Test with multiple holders to verify batch forced conversion works
Test whether the 1:1 FERC20-to-agentToken ratio is economically correct given the totalSupply bug in FERC20
